column1: Spanish Inquisition and Religious Persecution in Church History

column6: Additional Context
Established Nicene Christianity as the state religion of the Roman Empire, beginning the era of state enforcement of religious orthodoxy.

column4: Priscillian of Avila, Emperor Magnus Maximus
column6: First recorded execution of a heretic by Christian authorities. Priscillian was condemned for heresy and sorcery. Many church leaders, including Martin of Tours, objected to the execution.
Banned pagan practices and ordered the closure or destruction of pagan temples throughout the Roman Empire.
Mob murder of female Neoplatonist philosopher Hypatia by Christian parabalani, possibly instigated by Alexandria's patriarch. Symbolized growing religious intolerance.

column2: Closing of the Academy of Athens
column6: Justinian's closure of the last major pagan philosophical school, part of his campaign to enforce Christian orthodoxy.
Condemned the "Three Chapters" and various heresies, strengthening imperial control over religious doctrine.
Early example of organized suppression of heretical preachers in Western Europe.
First burning of heretics in medieval Western Europe. Canons of the cathedral school and others were accused of Manichaeism and executed.
First university in Europe, became center for the study of canon and Roman law that would later be used in inquisitorial procedures.
First systematic collection of canon law, provided legal framework for later inquisitorial procedures.
First clear description of dualist heretics in Western Europe who would become primary targets of the later Inquisition.

column4: Pope Lucius III, Emperor Frederick I
column6: Decretal that established procedures for identifying and combating heresy, sometimes considered the founding document of the medieval Inquisition.
Beginning of pontificate that would expand papal power and intensify the fight against heresy.

column4: Peter of Castelnau, Count Raymond VI of Toulouse
column6: Murder of papal legate that triggered the Albigensian Crusade against Cathar heretics in southern France.

column4: Simon de Montfort, Pope Innocent III
column6: Military campaign against Cathar heretics that resulted in thousands of deaths and established precedent for violent suppression of heresy.
Established requirement for annual confession, defined procedure for prosecuting heretics, mandated Jews and Muslims to wear distinctive clothing.
Formally established the Papal Inquisition, appointing the first "inquisitors of heretical depravity."
Dominicans appointed as the first official papal inquisitors in Toulouse, beginning specialized inquisitorial work.
Authorized the use of torture in inquisitorial proceedings, though with limitations. Marked a significant procedural development in the Inquisition.

column4: King Philip IV, Pope Clement V
column6: Mass arrest of Knights Templar on charges of heresy, apostasy, and various blasphemies. Many were tortured into confession.
Burning of mystical writer for heresy after refusing to recant her work or recognize the authority of the Inquisition.

column4: Jacques de Molay, King Philip IV
column6: Burning of the last Grand Master of the Knights Templar, ending the Templar trials which exemplified the use of inquisitorial methods for political purposes.
Equated sorcery with heresy, expanding inquisitorial jurisdiction to include witchcraft cases.
Execution of the last known Cathar perfectus, effectively ending the Cathar movement after a century of persecution.
Series of attacks against Jewish communities that created pressure for conversion and set the stage for the later Spanish Inquisition.
Violent anti-Jewish riots across Spain resulting in thousands of deaths and forced conversions, creating the converso population later targeted by the Spanish Inquisition.
Ended the Western Schism and condemned Jan Hus and John Wycliffe as heretics. Hus was burned at the stake despite having an imperial safe conduct.
Burning of Bohemian reformer for heresy, triggering the Hussite Wars. Became an important symbol for later Protestant movements.

column5: Various manifestos including the Four Articles of Prague
column6: Series of crusades against Hussite heretics in Bohemia, representing a large-scale military response to heresy.

column4: Joan of Arc, Bishop Pierre Cauchon
column6: Execution of the French military leader on charges of heresy and cross-dressing following an inquisitorial trial with political motivations.
Ottoman conquest that ended the Byzantine Empire and increased Western European fears of Muslim expansion, contributing to religious anxieties.
First major book printed with movable type in Europe, initiating a revolution in communication that would challenge church control of information.
Public ritual of the Spanish Inquisition in which penitents and heretics were brought forward for public penance or execution.
Violence against conversos (Jewish converts to Christianity) that increased pressure for establishing a formal inquisition to identify "false converts."

column4: Ferdinand II of Aragon, Isabella I of Castile, Pope Sixtus IV
column6: Papal bull authorizing the Spanish monarchs to appoint inquisitors within their domains, marking the beginning of the Spanish Inquisition.
Establishment of the first tribunal of the Spanish Inquisition in Seville.

column4: Miguel de Morillo, Juan de San Martín
column6: Six people burned at the first public auto-da-fé of the Spanish Inquisition.

column4: Tomás de Torquemada, Ferdinand and Isabella
column6: Centralization of the Spanish Inquisition under Torquemada, who established uniform procedures and expanded its reach.
Recognized the existence of witches and authorized inquisitors Heinrich Kramer and Jacob Sprenger to prosecute witchcraft in Germany.
Influential treatise on identifying and prosecuting witches, became a key text for witch hunts throughout Europe.
Forced approximately 200,000 Jews to convert or leave Spain, increasing the scrutiny of conversos by the Inquisition.
Forcible conversion of Jews in Portugal, creating a new population of crypto-Jews who would later be targeted by the Portuguese Inquisition.
Hanging and burning of the Dominican friar who had criticized church corruption and briefly ruled Florence as a theocracy.
Forced conversion of Granada's Muslims to Christianity, creating the Morisco population that would be scrutinized by the Inquisition.
Beginning of the Protestant Reformation, leading to intensified efforts against heresy in Catholic territories.
Papal bull threatening Luther with excommunication, followed by formal excommunication in 1521 with Decet Romanum Pontificem.
Imperial diet that declared Luther an outlaw and banned his writings throughout the Holy Roman Empire.
First executions of Protestants in the Low Countries, beginning decades of religious persecution.
Imperial troops pillaged Rome, seen by some as divine judgment on papal corruption and influencing religious politics.

column4: King João III, Pope Clement VII
column5: Papal bull Cum ad nihil magis
column6: Establishment of the Portuguese Inquisition modeled after the Spanish Inquisition, targeting primarily New Christians of Jewish origin.
Established the English monarch as head of the Church of England, beginning the English Reformation and subsequent persecution of Catholics.
Execution of Henry VIII's second wife on charges of treason, adultery, and incest, illustrating the intertwining of political and religious authority.

column2: Calvin's Institutes of the Christian Religion
column6: Publication of Calvin's systematic theology that would influence Protestant persecution of dissidents including Michael Servetus.
Papal bull establishing the Supreme Sacred Congregation of the Roman and Universal Inquisition (Holy Office) to combat Protestant heresy in Italy.
Beginning of formal proceedings by the Roman Inquisition against suspected Protestants in Italian states.

column4: Pope Paul III, Pope Julius III, Pope Pius IV
column6: Counter-Reformation council that clarified Catholic doctrine and strengthened institutional responses to Protestantism, including inquisitorial methods.
Burning of the Spanish theologian for denying the Trinity, showing that Protestant authorities also used execution to enforce religious orthodoxy.
Agreement allowing German princes to choose Catholicism or Lutheranism for their territories, established principle of cuius regio, eius religio (ruler determines religion).
Division of Habsburg territories between Philip II (Spain, Netherlands, Italian territories) and Ferdinand I (Austria, Holy Roman Empire).

column2: Establishment of Index of Forbidden Books
column6: First official index of books prohibited by the Catholic Church, a key tool of intellectual control.
Major public ceremony targeting Protestant circles in Spain, attended by the royal family and resulting in numerous executions.
Failed Protestant plot against the Catholic Guise family, intensifying religious tensions in France.
Series of civil wars between Catholics and Protestants culminating in the Edict of Nantes granting limited toleration to Huguenots.
Beginning of the Eighty Years' War triggered partly by Spanish attempts to suppress Protestantism in the Low Countries.

column4: Duke of Alba, Counts of Egmont and Hoorn
column6: Execution of Dutch nobles that intensified resistance to Spanish rule and religious persecution.

column4: Pope Pius V, Queen Elizabeth I
column6: Papal bull excommunicating Elizabeth I and releasing her subjects from allegiance, intensifying persecution of Catholics in England.
Mass killing of French Protestants (Huguenots) that shocked Europe and intensified religious conflicts.

column2: Union of Spanish and Portuguese crowns
column6: Personal union that brought Portugal under Spanish control, including coordination between the Spanish and Portuguese Inquisitions.

column2: Peak of witch hunt activity in Europe
column6: Period of most intensive witch prosecutions, often involving inquisitorial methods and torture.
Failed Spanish invasion attempt against England that weakened Spain's ability to enforce religious orthodoxy internationally.
Granted limited religious freedoms to French Protestants, temporarily ending the French Wars of Religion.
Burning of the philosopher for heresy by the Roman Inquisition after a seven-year trial, making him a symbol of intellectual freedom.
Failed Catholic conspiracy to blow up Parliament and kill King James I, leading to increased persecution of English Catholics.
Forced expulsion of approximately 300,000 Moriscos (Muslim converts to Christianity) from Spain, ending the main focus of the Spanish Inquisition on this group.

column2: Last execution of relapsed Jewish converts
column6: Burning of the last convicted "Judaizers" in Spain, as the Inquisition shifted focus to other offenses.
Roman Inquisition ordered Galileo to abandon Copernicanism, beginning his conflict with church authorities.
Devastating religious-political conflict that eventually established the principle of religious coexistence in the Holy Roman Empire.

column2: Establishment of the Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith
column6: Creation of the congregation tasked with missionary work, a shift toward conversion rather than coercion in spreading Catholicism.
Galileo forced to recant his support for heliocentrism and placed under house arrest, exemplifying the conflict between science and religious authority.
Conflict with strong religious dimensions leading to brief Puritan rule and religious persecution of Catholics and Anglican loyalists.

column3: Münster and Osnabrück, Holy Roman Empire
column6: End of the Thirty Years' War establishing the principle of state sovereignty in religious matters, weakening papal and imperial authority.
Expulsion of the philosopher from the Jewish community for heretical views, illustrating that religious persecution was not limited to Christian authorities.
Massive public ceremony of the Spanish Inquisition attended by the king, an elaborate demonstration of the institution's power.

column2: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes
column6: Ended religious toleration for French Protestants, leading to persecution and mass emigration of Huguenots.
Scientific work that contributed to the Enlightenment worldview that would eventually challenge the authority of religious institutions.
Colonial American witch panic influenced by European precedents and religious tensions.
Barred Catholics from the English throne, codifying religious discrimination in the constitutional order.
Spread of fraternal organization that would become a target of the Inquisition and papal condemnation for its secrecy and alleged irreligion.

column2: Last execution by the Spanish Inquisition
column6: Burning of a Crypto-Jew, marking the decline of the Inquisition's most severe punishments.
First papal bull explicitly condemning Freemasonry and excommunicating Catholic members.
Expulsion of the Jesuit order from Portugal and its colonies, reflecting growing secularism and anti-clerical sentiment.
Jesuit missionary executed for heresy and false prophecy, one of the last executions by the Portuguese Inquisition.
Execution of a Protestant accused of murdering his son to prevent conversion to Catholicism, became cause célèbre for Enlightenment critics of religious intolerance.
Expulsion of the Jesuit order from Spain and its colonies, reflecting royal absolutism and anticlerical trends.
Papal brief dissolving the Society of Jesus, responding to pressure from Catholic monarchs.
Established first modern secular state with constitutional protection for religious freedom.
Last execution by burning carried out by the Portuguese Inquisition.

column5: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
column6: Political upheaval that targeted the Catholic Church, confiscated church property, and persecuted clergy who refused the Civil Constitution of the Clergy.
Constitutional protection of religious freedom and prohibition of established religion in the United States.
Temporary end of papal temporal power and arrest of Pope Pius VI by French forces.

column2: Suppression of Spanish Inquisition under Joseph Bonaparte
column6: Temporary abolition of the Spanish Inquisition during Napoleonic rule of Spain.
Abolition of the Spanish Inquisition by Spain's liberal constitutional government.
Reestablishment of the Spanish Inquisition after the restoration of Ferdinand VII.
Papal bull reversing the 1773 suppression of the Jesuits.
Permanent abolition of the Spanish Inquisition following the liberal revolution of 1820.
Final dissolution of the Portuguese Inquisition following the Liberal Revolution of 1820.
Removed most legal disabilities for Catholics in the United Kingdom.
Final legal confirmation of the abolition of the Spanish Inquisition.

column5: Various encyclicals and the Syllabus of Errors
column6: Conservative papacy that issued the Syllabus of Errors condemning liberalism, modernism, and religious pluralism.
First exercise of papal infallibility, strengthening papal authority in doctrinal matters.
Controversy over papal authorities removing a Jewish boy from his family because he had been secretly baptized, causing international outrage.
Papal document condemning 80 "errors" of modern thought including rationalism, liberalism, and religious pluralism.
Council that defined papal infallibility, strengthening centralized control over doctrine.
End of the Papal States as the Italian unification movement captured Rome, confining papal sovereignty to the Vatican.
Papacy marked by diplomatic engagement with the modern world while maintaining doctrinal conservatism.
Political scandal involving false accusation of treason against a Jewish army officer, supported by conservative Catholic elements in France.
Papacy marked by strong opposition to theological modernism and required loyalty oaths from clergy.
Renamed the former Inquisition as the "Supreme Sacred Congregation of the Holy Office" with continued responsibility for doctrinal matters.
Required oath of loyalty from all priests opposing modernist theological trends, creating atmosphere of suspicion and self-censorship.

column2: Promulgation of the Code of Canon Law
column6: First comprehensive codification of Catholic canon law, standardizing church discipline and procedures.
Papacy that established Vatican City as a sovereign state and navigated the rise of totalitarian regimes.

column4: John T. Scopes, William Jennings Bryan
column6: Famous trial over teaching evolution, highlighting ongoing tension between religious authority and scientific education.
Established Vatican City as a sovereign state and regularized church-state relations in Italy.
Controversial agreement between the Holy See and Nazi Germany protecting church rights while legitimizing the Nazi regime.
Papal encyclical condemning Nazi ideology and violations of the concordat, smuggled into Germany and read from pulpits.
Organized pogrom against Jews in Germany, met with limited Vatican protest.
Controversial papacy during World War II and the Holocaust, criticized for insufficient public opposition to Nazi atrocities.
Genocide of six million Jews and millions of others, raised questions about the response of Christian churches to antisemitism.
Ambiguous reference to victims of war interpreted by some as insufficient condemnation of the Holocaust.
Defeat of Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan, beginning of reckoning with religious complicity in fascism.

column2: Declaration of the State of Israel
column6: Establishment of Jewish state, challenging traditional Catholic anti-Zionism and theology regarding the Jewish people.
Exercise of papal infallibility to define the dogma of Mary's bodily assumption into heaven.

column2: First "Stalinist" show trials in Eastern Europe
column6: Political trials with similarities to inquisitorial procedures, targeting Catholic clergy among others.
Surprising announcement of a council to "open the windows" of the Church to the modern world.

column4: Pope John XXIII, Pope Paul VI
column5: Various council documents including Dignitatis Humanae
column6: Council that modernized Catholic practices and explicitly endorsed religious freedom, marking historic shift from inquisitorial attitudes.
Vatican II document affirming the right to religious freedom, repudiating centuries of Catholic teaching on religious coer
